headlines.title: Recap: USA beats Bosnia and Herzegovina 2-0 in World Cup Round of 32
headlines.description: What to Know The U.S. men’s national team eliminated Bosnia and Herzegovina in the World Cup Round of 32 despite playing with 10 men for well over half a hour. Awaiting the U.S. in the Round of 16 is Belgium after the Red Devils rallied down 2-0 to stun Senegal 3-2 deep in extra time. Folarin Balogun, moments after having an offsides goal, secured the opener in the 45th minute after a deflected through ball from Malik Tillman fell in his path. Balogun was later issued a red card in the 64th minute for serious foul play on what appeared to be an incidental leg-landing challenge on defender Tarik Muharemovic. Tillman gave the U.S. a key cushion by scoring a stunning free kick in the 82nd minute to make it 2-0 despite being down a man. USA entered the game ranked No. 15 by FIFA, while Bosnia and Herzegovina came in at No. 61. The Stars and Stripes topped Group D with wins over Paraguay and Australia before falling to Turkey on a late goal with a rotated team. The Dragons finished third in Group B with a tie vs. Canada, a big loss to Switzerland and a win over Qatar. This live blog on the USMNT-Bosnia and Herzegovina World Cup game has ended. Follow here for more coverage.

headlines.title: Construction work appears to have caused fire at historic SF church
headlines.description: A fire that tore through a historic church in San Franciso on Monday appears to have been caused by construction work on the building, according to fire investigators. Wednesday, leaders talked about the next steps as they work to pick up the pieces. The smoke has cleared, but there is a long road ahead after a fire gutted the San Francisco Central Seventh Day Adventist Church. “I’m heartbroken, this church means a lot to me and my family,” Senior pastor Mark Ferrell said. Ferrell has been a senior pastor at the church for 20 years. He says the fire started on the upper level near the windows. “When the roof burned it fell down into the main worship area, which is up in the mezzanine level, and so the inside has a lot of waterlogged pews, a whole bunch of burned timbers,” he said. Work is underway to make the building safe after the fire. It was built in 1892 by shipbuilders. Over the past few years, the church has done some resurfacing of the sandstone and work to seal the windows to keep water out. More upgrades were underway Monday when someone spotted flames. “About 1:30 p.m., 2 p.m, there was a fire that was started and the fire department was quickly called by the construction teams,” Ferrell said. More than 100 firefighters responded and faced challenges including the buildings age, open space and wind. Firefighters were able to use drone technology with thermal imaging capability to help direct crews. By night the fire was under control, and five people were displaced in another building. “Fire investigators are still continuing to sift through some of the rubble and right their report but their initial reports for sure are the construction initially caused the fire,” Mariano Elis with the SFFD said. No one was hurt. Those with the church say they’re grateful to firefighters and all who helped and reached out. “This is a church that has been serving this community for close to a hundred years,” Central California Conference of the Seventh-Day Adventist Church President Ricardo Viloria said. They’re thinking of their building and their community. They will continue with services online and made plans for this week’s services. “This coming sabbath we’re going to be joining the San Francisco Philadelphian Church which is two blocks away,” Ferrill said. “I know that this church is going to come back from the ashes, I know that this church is going to come back stronger, I know that this church is going to rebuild,” Viloria said.

headlines.title: SFDA announces charges in connection with incidents during Pride weekend
headlines.description: San Francisco’s district attorney on Wednesday announced felony charges against several people in connection with separate incidents, including vandalism, that happened during Pride weekend. A melee at the end of last Friday’s Trans March resulted in four people being arrested and charged. According to District Attorney Brooke Jenkins, the confrontation happened, in part, because officers were trying to arrest a pair who they say had been spray-painting pink messages on private property, city-operated Flock cameras and neighborhood security cameras. “This was pre-planned activity,” Jenkins said. “This was something that these individuals set out to do. They were prepared. They had an agenda, which was to disrupt law enforcement’s ability to do its job, which is why they targeted surveillance cameras and Flock cameras.” The arrests happened where the march finished, just outside the former Compton’s Cafeteria historic site in the city’s Tenderloin neighborhood. During Tuesday’s board of supervisors meeting, Supervisor Jackie Fielder made reference to that when she announced a letter of inquiry to the police department over its handling of the situation. San Francisco Jun 28 San Francisco Jun 28 “The images of police in full tactical gear harken back to the Compton’s Cafeteria riot of 1966 in which trans women and drag queens were protesting police brutality,” Fielder said. “I have questions about the decision making to deploy officers in full tactical gear.” According to Jenkins, while officers were trying to arrest two people for the vandalism, several others tried to prevent those arrests. That’s when two others were taken into custody and charged with resisting or obstructing the police. Jenkins said one of them spit on an officer. “We need to be clear, again, to flesh out all of the facts,” Jenkins said. “People are still doing that. But some of the facts have been left out of the discussion, which is the conduct that now has been charged, of people spitting on police.” A shooting on Saturday and a stabbing on Sunday were not necessarily connected to Pride events, Jenkins said.

headlines.title: Firefighter's insurance struggles has Mayor Lurie concerned about thousands of other city employees, retirees
headlines.description: San Francisco Mayor Daniel Lurie says Blue Shield of California’s decision to deny cancer treatment for a longtime firefighter late last year now has him concerned about thousands of other city employees and retirees who maintain their city-backed health insurance plans through Blue Shield. The insurance company tells NBC Bay Area it’s committed to providing San Francisco employees, retirees, and their families with high-quality, affordable care. Senior Investigative Reporter Bigad Shaban has more.

headlines.title: World Cup brings economy boost to the Bay Area
headlines.description: The World Cup has been really good for business, as it brought people from all over the world to the Bay Area. The Silicon Valley Chamber of Commerce says it expects somewhere between $500 million and $650 million to come into local cash registers because of the World Cup. “What really inspires me is this opportunity we’ve had to put the Bay Area on the world stage and tell our story. I think most of the folks who come here, this won’t be their last time visiting our wonderful region,” Silicon Valley Leadership Group CEO Ahmad Thomas said. Scott Budman has the full report in the video above.

headlines.title: 4th of July safety: Bay Area firefighters add extra crews, urge public to be responsible
headlines.description: Authorities are taking proactive steps to help prevent fires and keep communities safe during the Fourth of July holiday. In Alameda County, officials points to the dry fire conditions and are urging the public to celebrate the holiday responsibly. “With the hot and dry conditions that we have, any type of spark could start one of those fires. Obviously, with anything getting in alignment with fuel, weather and topography could cause those fires to grow exponentially,” Alameda County Fire Department Capt. Aaron Lee said. “We want our communities to be safe.” Officials are also cracking down on illegal fireworks. Alameda County Sheriff’s Office deputies last week arrested a wanted felon and seized more than 500 pounds of commercial-grade fireworks. Another 4,300 pounds of fireworks were confiscated in a separate case last month in Oakland. Fourth of July Jun 26, 2025 San Francisco Jun 30 Fourth of July Jun 29 Starting Thursday, a new county ordinance bans all fireworks in unincorporated Alameda County. Violators could face fines from $500 to $1,000, and responsibility will not fall only on those lighting the fireworks. “That will hold social host of events accountable for guest that are setting any type of fireworks off on their property or public right away adjacent to their property,” Alameda County Sheriff’s Office Sgt. Kurt Imperial said. The Alameda County Fire Department is pre-positioning additional crews and equipment in anticipating of more fire calls during the holiday. The department is adding four fire engines, extra water resources and a hand crew with 20 additional firefighters. “The goal of increasing our staffing is to make sure we have units available, placed in the right spots and ready to fight fire aggressively,” Alameda County Fire Department Deputy Chief Ryan Nishimoto said. Extra resources will be strategically staged in the Alameda County cities where safe and sane fireworks are legal: Dublin, Newark and Union City. Strike teams have also been taking proactive steps to reduce fuel loads. “Although we can’t effect weather, we can effect topographic, we can effect the fuels,” said Eric Truax, an Alameda County Fire Department hand crew captain. “These give firefighters a foothold for out of control wildfires putting in pre-established fuel breaks.”

headlines.title: Protesters in Santa Clara rally against proposed ICE facility near Gilroy
headlines.description: A group of protesters fighting a proposed ICE facility in Gilroy staged their demonstration right on the street where fans walk to go to San Francisco Bay Area Stadium ahead of Wednesday’s U.S.-Bosnia-Herzegovina match. The “ICE out of the Bay” coalition community staged their protest on Tasman Drive, the street to the stadium, to voice their opposition to an ICE facility being built near Gilroy. They say blueprints indicate the building includes a detention and processing center. The state, Santa Clara County and numerous cities are trying to stop the project and protesters are trying to support those efforts. They said the World Cup was the perfect stage for their message. “This is a perfect place to reach the world. We want ICE to get out of Gilroy. We want ICE to get out of Dublin. We want them out of California, out of everywhere. There’s no need for this type of enforcement. There’s no need for this type of lack of humanity,” Rebecca Armendariz with the ICE Out of the Bay coalition said. The group has now moved It’s protest to other areas around the stadium. It’s important to note that the ICE facility is not being built within the Gilroy city limits, so the city did not have control over the approval process. ICE has not confirmed the facility is a detention center, but blueprints shown publicly indicate there is detention and processing areas within the office building.

headlines.title: Newsom signs bill making May 17 official Bruce Lee Day in California
headlines.description: Gov. Gavin Newsom signed a bill Tuesday officially recognizing May 17 as Bruce Lee Day in California, honoring the San Francisco-born actor and cultural figure. The commemorative day will make Bruce Lee the first Chinese American to be honored with a day in California, according to Assemblymember Matt Haney, who introduced the bill in February “Bruce Lee represents the very best of California: innovation, diversity, determination, and the courage to challenge convention,” Haney said in a press release announcing the signing of the bill. “Born in San Francisco, he transformed martial arts, redefined Hollywood, and inspired millions around the world to pursue their potential. At a time when Asian Americans were too often absent from or stereotyped on screen, Bruce Lee helped generations see themselves represented with strength and dignity.” Lee was born in San Francisco in 1940 and raised in Hong Kong. He became a child actor in Chinese films and later became an international star most known for martial arts. Haney’s press release said he returned to San Francisco at the age of 18 on May 17, 1959, to expand his career and global impact. Lee became a cultural icon and filmmaker who became known for his philosophy of adaptability, discipline and self-expression, the release said. “To be the first Chinese American recognized this way is a testament to my father’s enduring legacy and the countless lives he continues to touch.” Shannon Lee, founder of the Bruce Lee Foundation and Bruce Lee’s daughter, said in the release. “From young people who found confidence and possibility in his philosophy, to families who finally saw themselves represented on screen, to athletes who still draw on his teachings of discipline and inner strength, his reach is profound. My father was a bridge between cultures through his courage, and his spirit of interracial solidarity remains as relevant as ever.” The law’s text said the commemorative day will encourage school lessons, cultural exhibits and public events to highlight Lee’s impact and accomplishments. The Chinese Historical Society of America is currently displaying its “We are Bruce Lee: Under the Sky, One Family” exhibit at its museum in San Francisco’s Chinatown. Haney said he hopes the annual day of commemoration will inspire people from any background to “have the power to change the world.” The first Bruce Lee Day in California will be celebrated on May 17, 2027.

headlines.title: Bay Area Host Committee announces plans to become permanent sports leadership commission
headlines.description: The Bay Area Host Committee, which helped coordinate the World Cup and Super Bowl in the region, has announced plans to make the committee a permanent regional leadership commission that will continue to bring sports events to the Bay Area for years to come. The initiative announced Tuesday will be led by its inaugural Leadership Council, which will bring together some of the Bay Area’s biggest executives, politicians and businessmen to create a cohort that will continue the committee’s work to bring major sports events to the region. Speaking on the committee’s future, BAHC officials voiced excitement to bid for more sports events in the future — like the 2031 FIFA Women’s World Cup, which will be hosted by the United States, Mexico, Costa Rica and Jamaica — with the hope of building upon the momentum created over the last two calendar years. “We’ve proven the Bay Area can execute as a unified region on the world’s biggest stage,” Larry Baer, who serves on the BAHC’s Board of Directors as president and CEO of the San Francisco Giants, said in a statement. “The Leadership Council ensures we preserve the relationships, expertise and momentum we’ve built, and it positions us to write the next chapter of Bay Area sports history.” Following the committee’s announcement of its permanent leadership cohort, the BAHC joined San Francisco and U.S. soccer leaders Tuesday night to watch the Mexico-Ecuador match in downtown San Francisco. The watch party celebrated the work the BAHC has done to bring major sporting events to the region, coming one night before the Bay Area will host its final World Cup match, a round of 32 bout between the United States and Bosnia-Herzegovina in Santa Clara. Gathered together, regional leaders and event organizers remarked upon what has been a landmark summer for the Bay Area, highlighted by increases in tourism and economic activity. In May, SF Travel, the official marketing organization of San Francisco, projected the city will welcome 24.2 million visitors in 2026, exceeding its 2025 mark of 23.7 million. SF Travel also projected visitor spending will reach $9.9 billion, which would exceed the city’s pre-pandemic record of $9.6 billion in 2019. The BAHC also projects that the NBA All-Star Weekend held in San Francisco in 2025, combined with the Super Bowl in February and the World Cup matches for the past month, will bring $1.6 billion in economic activity for the region. San Francisco Mayor Daniel Lurie, who previously led the San Francisco Bay Area Super Bowl 50 Host Committee when Levi’s Stadium hosted the big football game in 2016, was especially complimentary of the impact the BAHC’s events have had on his city. “The eyes of the world are on us,” said Lurie, speaking on how the World Cup showcases San Francisco and the Bay Area to a global audience. “Visitors from all over the world come here for a few days, and then they go back, they report to their friends and family and they’re like, ‘Oh, we gotta go visit San Francisco in the Bay Area again.'” The mayor’s office has worked to capitalize on this tourism to fuel the city’s revitalization, outlining safety, transportation and cleanliness as city priorities ahead of the World Cup. Regional leaders and organizers also celebrated the collaboration required to bring major sporting events to the Bay Area, highlighting partnerships among local governments, business leaders and sports organizations “I need local government,” said Zaileen Janmohamed, president and CEO of the BAHC, in an interview alongside Lurie. “I need all the sports teams to come together to really pull this thing off, because it literally has not been done before and it doesn’t work unless everyone comes together to make it happen.” Moving forward, the BAHC will aim to capitalize upon these established connections, with the intent to help the Bay Area host one major sports event every two or three years moving forward.

headlines.title: San Francisco — 250 years of boom and bust
headlines.description: Even before San Francisco became San Francisco — or really anything more than endless sand dunes — people arrived seeking fortune. Two hundred and fifty years ago, on June 27, 1776, the Anza Expedition wrapped up a 1,200 mile trek, landing in future San Francisco with the promise of land ownership. Their arrival was a tone-setter for the next 250 years. Less than a century later, the cry of gold drew many more dreamers to the burgeoning city hugging the bay. “It comes in existence in this enormous boom,” said author and historian Gary Kamiya. “And then it has these cataclysmic booms and bust throughout its whole history.” For Lance Beeson, a descendant of Lt. José Joaquin Moraga, who led the Anza Expedition the final leg of its journey, the colonizers did what people who make the pilgrimage to San Francisco have always done. “There is a commonality present in the history of San Francisco, is people come here seeking their fortune,” Beeson said. “And that’s exactly what they did.” Nearly a century after the arrival of the Anza Expedition in San Francisco, the cry of gold lured thousands to San Francisco. It’s fitting San Francisco’s official shield features a mythological phoenix. The city burned down several times even before the 1906 earthquake and fire leveled the place. By then it had also rebounded from a financial collapse in the 1870s brought on by the Panic of 1873. After the 1906 disaster, the city rebuilt itself once again — hosting the 1915 Panama Pacific International Exposition to celebrate the completion of the Panama Canal, as well as the city’s own tenacity. “The boom and bust cycles continue,” said Kamiya. “The city rebuilds, becomes very prosperous, then it goes into the Great Depression like the rest of the country. And then we have the successions of the dot-com booms and busts.” San Francisco Jun 30 San Francisco Jun 29 Remember Web Van? The food delivery service touted as a symbol for the dot-com boom and bust in the early 1990s? The liquidation sale included basketball hoops, ping pong tables alongside multi-thousand dollar office chairs. The bust hit with a pretty significant thud. A second dot-com boom — followed by a precipitous pandemic plummet. Now AI has scribbled yet another financial peak. Who knows what comes next? “San Francisco has sort of a character of repeated sort of attempts at raising your fortunes,” opined Kari Jones, archaeologist at the Presidio, where the Anza party settled. Beyond its peaks and valleys, San Francisco has always been a place that attracted thinkers and dreamers, doers and shakers. Television was born here. Sourdough bread, too. The LGBT movement. Levi’s jeans. Poets and politicians. Bankers and beats. Jack Kerouac described the area as the end of the continent where nobody gave a damn. A license to aim big. “San Francisco has a deep strain of being intensely individualistic,” said Kamiya. “It was founded by people that didn’t have to deal with the manners and mores of the East Coast when they came out here.” The Palace of Fine Arts was built for the 1915 Panama Pacific International Exposition, a celebration of San Francisco’s return from the 1906 earthquake and fire. Even the arrival of the Anza Expedition 250 years ago signaled a mixed blessing of sorts — while the colonists found prosperity, the indigenous tribes living in the area saw their way of life stamped out. There’s never been a pure payoff in San Francisco without a rub. “I think we should all be thankful for the fact that Anza and his men discovered this,” said Kamiya, sweeping his gaze along the Financial District skyline. “The tragedy of the native people notwithstanding.”

headlines.title: Did the milk expire? California bans ‘sell by' food labels to cut food waste
headlines.description: In Kimberley Kausen’s home, a passed “sell by” date on a jug of milk means different things to different family members. For her daughter, it means the jug belongs in the trash. For her husband, it means the milk is still good for a few more days. Kausen, a chef and cooking teacher in Irvine, California, is more discerning and often uses her sense of smell before deciding what to do with the milk. “I’ll put some thought into it, and if we’re talking about meat and poultry, I’m very cautious about that and for sure will do the smell test and the touch test,” she said. The debate playing out in Kausen’s kitchen is repeated in homes across California and the country, where varying phrases on food packaging have long left shoppers unsure whether food is simply past its peak quality or unsafe to eat. The state is aiming to cut down on confusion — and the food waste it creates when people throw away food early — with a new food labeling law starting Wednesday. It bans the use of “sell by” labels on food packaging, which experts say act as a guide for retailers on how long to display products on the shelves but are not an indicator of whether they are still safe to consume. Now, manufacturers selling food in California must use two standardized labels — a “Best if Used By” label for peak quality and “Use By” label for product safety. Food manufacturers can choose to use either label or both, said Democratic Assemblymember Jacqui Irwin, the author of the bill. California became the first state in the U.S. to standardize food labels when it approved the law in 2024 that seeks to reduce food waste and the state’s climate-warming emissions. New York state lawmakers recently approved a similar law that is awaiting Gov. Kathy Hochul’s signature. Legislation addressing food labeling also has been proposed in Illinois, Maryland, Massachusetts, New Jersey and South Carolina, though it has not passed in those states. Nick Lapis, director of advocacy at Californians Against Waste, which co-sponsored the bill, said food labels are the leading cause of household food waste. The “sell by” date labels have also been a problem for food banks in California because people consider those dates as meaning the food has expired, he said. “We don’t need to build some kind of huge infrastructure and invest tons of money to solve this. We just need companies to use the same words across brands,” he said. There are more than 50 different date labels on packaged food sold in stores, according to a 2022 report on food waste published by the University of Maryland. The information in the labels is largely unregulated and often does not relate to food safety. “Consumers get confused and they just default to assuming that whatever date is on the package means ‘don’t eat it and throw it away’,” said Kumar Chandran, policy director at ReFED, a nonprofit focused on reducing food waste. Chandran said California and New York’s approval of food-labeling laws has added momentum to the push for a national standard. A bipartisan bill that would establish uniform food labels is pending in Congress. The U.S. Department of Agriculture recommended a decade ago that food sellers should switch to “Best if Used By” labeling. Currently, the only product that is regulated federally with date labels is infant formula. With no federal regulations dictating what information labels should include, the stamps have led to consumer confusion — and nearly 20% of the nation’s food waste, according to the Food and Drug Administration. In California, that’s about 6 million tons of unexpired food that’s tossed in the trash each year. Nate Rose, a spokesperson for the California Grocers Association, said some grocers have had to overhaul their labeling systems, but as a whole, the association has been supportive of the change. The new labels will result in “a win-win where we can reduce food waste and consumers will find these decisions a little bit simpler,” he said, adding that shoppers will still find old labels in stores for months to come as grocers sell through the products that already have them.
headlines.source: Olga R. Rodriguez | The Associated Press

headlines.title: 2 shot, wounded after San Francisco World Cup watch party
headlines.description: Two people were shot after a fight near a World Cup watch party in San Francisco’s Mission Bay neighborhood Tuesday night. The shooting happened around 9 p.m. near Mission Bay Boulevard and Fourth Street. Video from the scene showed crime tape surrounding Spark Social SF, which had hosted a World Cup watch party earlier in the evening. The San Francisco Chronicle reported an argument broke out during the final minutes of a DJ set before a man pulled out a gun and opened fire. Officers found two people suffering from gunshot wounds. According to the Chronicle, both victims were taken to a hospital and are expected to survive. The suspected shooter fled the scene. No arrests had been made as of Wednesday morning.
